sourceMovie.title: Spy
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Spy (2015) stuck because it commits to its action premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Paul Feig's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The action and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: RED
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Spy's action stayed with you, RED (2010) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The comedy through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: 2 Guns
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Spy excels in action, 2 Guns (2013) doubles down on comedy to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: RED 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: RED 2 (2013) is the natural next step after Spy: same action DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and comedy elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Spy who respond to its action will find The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent (2022) immediately familiar. Both films share a comedy sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: The Man from U.N.C.L.E.
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Spy fans keep landing on The Man from U.N.C.L.E. (2015) — the action is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Knight and Day
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Knight and Day draws from the same action well as Spy, channeling comedy in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2010 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Charlie's Angels
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Charlie's Angels (2019) captures the action appeal of Spy without simply imitating it. The comedy streak running through both films is what puts Charlie's Angels at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Swordfish
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action appeal of Spy — that dark edge, the crime — translates cleanly into Swordfish (2001). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: From Paris with Love
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind From Paris with Love (2010) clearly understood what makes action work — the same quality that keeps Spy rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Team America: World Police
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Team America: World Police (2004) is the kind of film Spy fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The action overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

faq.question: What should I watch after Spy?
faq.answer: Try RED, 2 Guns, RED 2—each shares DNA with Spy while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Spy?
faq.answer: Spy (2015) is best described as Action, Comedy, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Spy worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.8/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this action lane.
